**Maundy Thursday**, also referred to as **Holy Thursday**, or **Thursday of the Lord's Supper**, among other names,[[note 1]](#cite_note-1) is a [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") feast during [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week") that marks the beginning of the [Paschal Triduum](/wiki/Paschal_Triduum "Paschal Triduum"), and commemorates the [Washing of the Feet](/wiki/Washing_of_the_Feet "Washing of the Feet") (Maundy) and [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper") of [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ") with [the Apostles](/wiki/Apostles_in_the_New_Testament "Apostles in the New Testament"), as described in the [canonical gospels](/wiki/Canonical_gospels "Canonical gospels").[[1]](#cite_note-Story-2)

It is the fifth day of [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week"), preceded by [Holy Wednesday](/wiki/Holy_Wednesday "Holy Wednesday") (Spy Wednesday) and followed by [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday").[[2]](#cite_note-3) "Maundy" comes from the Latin word *mandatum*, or commandment, reflecting Jesus' words "I give you a new commandment."[[3]](#cite_note-4)
The date of the day will vary according to whether the [Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar") or the [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar") is used. Eastern churches generally use the Julian system.

## Names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maundy_Thursday&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Names")]

The *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")* of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), which is the mother Church of the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"), uses the name "Maundy Thursday" for this observance.[[4]](#cite_note-Table-5) The corresponding publication of the [US](/wiki/United_States "United States") [Episcopal Church](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)"), which is another province of the Anglican Communion, also refers to the Thursday before Easter as "Maundy Thursday".[[5]](#cite_note-6) Throughout the Anglican Communion, the term "Holy Thursday" is a synonym for [Ascension Day](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Ascension "Feast of the Ascension").[[4]](#cite_note-Table-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Forster-7)[[7]](#cite_note-Soane-8)

The [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite") of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") uses the name "Holy Thursday" in its modern English-language liturgical books.[[8]](#cite_note-GIRM-9) The Latin books use the name *Feria quinta in Cena Domini* ("Thursday of the Supper of the Lord"; the [medieval](/wiki/Medieval_Latin "Medieval Latin") spelling *[Cœna](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/coena#Latin "wikt:coena")* was used in place of *[Cena](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/cena#Latin "wikt:cena")* in documents predating the 1955 decree [Maxima redemptionis](/wiki/Liturgical_reforms_of_Pope_Pius_XII "Liturgical reforms of Pope Pius XII")), along with Maundy Thursday as the English name, as given in *The Saint Andrew Daily Missal*.[[9]](#cite_note-Lefebvre1999-10) The [personal ordinariates](/wiki/Personal_ordinariate "Personal ordinariate") in the Catholic Church, which have an Anglican patrimony, retain the traditional English term "Maundy Thursday", however.[[10]](#cite_note-11) An article in the 1911 *[Catholic Encyclopedia](/wiki/Catholic_Encyclopedia "Catholic Encyclopedia")* used the term "Maundy Thursday",[[11]](#cite_note-12) and some Catholic writers use the same term either primarily,[[12]](#cite_note-Authors2008-13) or alternatively.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

The Methodist *[Book of Worship for Church and Home](/wiki/Book_of_Worship_for_Church_and_Home_(1965) "Book of Worship for Church and Home (1965)")* (1965) uses the term "Maundy Thursday";[[14]](#cite_note-BOW1964-15) the *[Book of Worship](/wiki/The_United_Methodist_Book_of_Worship_(1992) "The United Methodist Book of Worship (1992)")* (1992) uses the term "Holy Thursday",[[15]](#cite_note-UMBW-16) and other official sources of the [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church") use both "Maundy Thursday"[[16]](#cite_note-UMC_GBOD-Maundy_Thursday-17) and "Holy Thursday".[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-19)

Both names are used by other [Christian denominations](/wiki/Christian_denominations "Christian denominations") as well, including the [Lutheran Church](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"),[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21) and certain [Reformed Churches](/wiki/Reformed_Church "Reformed Church").[[21]](#cite_note-RCA-Maundy_Thursday-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-Calendar-24) Certain [Presbyterian Churches](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church "Presbyterian Church") use the term "Maundy Thursday" to refer to the holy day in their official sources.[[23]](#cite_note-Calendar-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25)

In the [Byzantine Rite](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite") the name for the holy day is "Great and Holy Thursday"[[25]](#cite_note-EOC-Name-26) or "Holy Thursday",[[26]](#cite_note-McKinney-27)[[27]](#cite_note-Metropolitan_Philip-28) and in [Western Rite Orthodoxy](/wiki/Western_Rite_Orthodoxy "Western Rite Orthodoxy") "Maundy Thursday",[[28]](#cite_note-Western_Orthodox-Maundy-29)[[29]](#cite_note-Western_Orthodox_CA-Maundy-30) "Holy Thursday"[[30]](#cite_note-Basil-31) or both.[[31]](#cite_note-32) The [Coptic Orthodox Church](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church "Coptic Orthodox Church") uses the term "Covenant Thursday" or "Thursday of the Covenant".[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34)[[34]](#cite_note-35)[[35]](#cite_note-36)[[36]](#cite_note-37)

In the [Maronite Church](/wiki/Maronite_Church "Maronite Church")[[37]](#cite_note-38) and the [Syriac Orthodox Church](/wiki/Syriac_Orthodox_Church "Syriac Orthodox Church"),[[38]](#cite_note-39) the name is "Thursday of Mysteries".

"Maundy Thursday" is the official name of the day in the civil legislation of England[[39]](#cite_note-40) and the Philippines.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

The day has also been known in English as *Shere Thursday* (also spelled *Sheer Thursday*), from the word *shere* (meaning "clean" or "bright").[[41]](#cite_note-Sheer-42) This name might refer to the act of cleaning, or to the fact that churches would switch [liturgical colors](/wiki/Liturgical_colors "Liturgical colors") from the dark tones of [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"), or because it was customary to shear the beard on that day,[[42]](#cite_note-43) or for a combination of reasons.[[43]](#cite_note-44) This name has cognates in the Nordic languages, such as Danish *skærtorsdag*, Swedish *skärtorsdag*, Norwegian *skjærtorsdag*, Faroese *skírhósdagur* and *skírisdagur*, Icelandic *skírdagur*, and Finnish *kiirastorstai*.

### Derivation of the name "Maundy"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maundy_Thursday&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Derivation of the name \"Maundy\"")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Maundy_Thursday_07_washing_feet_diocese_St_Asaph.jpg/250px-Maundy_Thursday_07_washing_feet_diocese_St_Asaph.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maundy_Thursday_07_washing_feet_diocese_St_Asaph.jpg)

Maundy ceremony in a [Church in Wales](/wiki/Church_in_Wales "Church in Wales") parish church during a Maundy Thursday [service of worship](/wiki/Church_service "Church service")

*[Maundy](/wiki/Footwashing "Footwashing")* is the name of the Christian rite of footwashing,[[44]](#cite_note-Bower2003-45) which traditionally occurs during Maundy Thursday church services.[[44]](#cite_note-Bower2003-45)

The English word *[maundy](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/maundy "wikt:maundy")* in the name for the day is derived through [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") and [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") *mandé*, from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *mandatum* (also the origin of the English word "mandate"), the first word of the phrase "*Mandatum novum do vobis ut diligatis invicem sicut dilexi vos*" ("A new commandment I give to you: That you love one another, as I have loved you.") By this statement in [chapter 13 of the Gospel of John](/wiki/John_13 "John 13"), Jesus explained to the Apostles the significance of his action of washing their feet.

The phrase is used as the [antiphon](/wiki/Antiphon "Antiphon") sung in the [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite") during the Maundy ([Ecclesiastical Latin](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_Latin "Ecclesiastical Latin"): "Mandatum") ceremony of the washing of the feet, which may be held during Mass or as a separate event. A priest or bishop, representing Christ, ceremonially washes the feet of others, typically 12 persons chosen as a cross-section of the community. In 2016, it was announced that the [Roman Missal](/wiki/Roman_Missal "Roman Missal") had been revised to allow women to participate as part of the 12 in the Mandatum. Previously, only males partook of the rite.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

Others theorize that the English name "Maundy Thursday" arose from "maunds" or baskets or "maundy purses" of alms which the king of England distributed to certain poor at Whitehall before attending Mass on that day. Thus, "maund" is connected to the Latin *mendicare*, and French *mendier*, to beg.[[46]](#cite_note-Schaff-47)

Maundy Thursday is sometimes erroneously called "Easter Thursday" (which properly refers to the [Thursday after Easter](/wiki/Octave_of_Easter "Octave of Easter")), especially in Australia, where the [Australian Football League](/wiki/Australian_Football_League "Australian Football League") regularly has an "Easter Thursday" game.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

## Services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maundy_Thursday&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Services")]

### Western Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maundy_Thursday&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Western Christianity")]

Maundy Thursday initiates the [Paschal Triduum](/wiki/Paschal_Triduum "Paschal Triduum"), the period which commemorates the [passion](/wiki/Passion_(Christianity) "Passion (Christianity)"), [death](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus"), and [resurrection of Jesus](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus"). This period includes Good Friday and [Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday"), and ends on the evening of Easter Sunday.[[1]](#cite_note-Story-2)[[48]](#cite_note-49) The [Mass of the Lord's Supper](/wiki/Mass_of_the_Lord%27s_Supper "Mass of the Lord's Supper") or service of worship is normally celebrated in the evening, when Friday begins according to Jewish tradition, as the Last Supper was held on the feast of [Passover](/wiki/Passover "Passover"), according to the three [Synoptic Gospels](/wiki/Synoptic_Gospels "Synoptic Gospels").[[49]](#cite_note-50)

#### Services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maundy_Thursday&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Services")]

Main articles: [Mass of the Lord's Supper](/wiki/Mass_of_the_Lord%27s_Supper "Mass of the Lord's Supper") and [Royal Maundy](/wiki/Royal_Maundy "Royal Maundy")

In the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), the [Royal Maundy](/wiki/Royal_Maundy "Royal Maundy") service involves the [Monarch](/wiki/Monarch "Monarch") offering "alms" to deserving senior citizens – one man and one woman for each year of the sovereign's age.[[50]](#cite_note-51) These coins, known as [Maundy money](/wiki/Maundy_money "Maundy money") or Royal Maundy, are distributed in red and white purses, and is a custom dating back to [King Edward I](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England"). The red purse contains regular currency and is given in place of food and clothing. The white purse has money in the amount of one penny for each year of the Sovereign's age. Since 1822, rather than ordinary money, the Sovereign gives out [Maundy coins](/wiki/Maundy_money#Maundy_coinage "Maundy money"),[[51]](#cite_note-52) which are specially minted 1, 2, 3 and 4 penny pieces, and are legal tender. The service at which this takes place rotates around English and Welsh churches, though in 2008 it took place for the first time in [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") at [Armagh Cathedral](/wiki/St_Patrick%27s_Cathedral,_Armagh_(Church_of_Ireland) "St Patrick's Cathedral, Armagh (Church of Ireland)"). Until the death of King [James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England"), the [Monarch](/wiki/Monarch "Monarch") would also wash the feet of the selected poor people. There is an old sketch, done from life, of Queen [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_England "Elizabeth I of England") washing people's feet on Maundy Thursday.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Maundy](/wiki/Footwashing "Footwashing") (washing of the feet) is practised among many Christian groups on Maundy Thursday, including the [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican")/[Protestant Episcopal](/wiki/Protestant_Episcopal_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America "Protestant Episcopal Church in the United States of America"),[[52]](#cite_note-53) [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_Apostolic_Church "Armenian Apostolic Church"),[[53]](#cite_note-54) [Ethiopian](/wiki/Ethiopian_Orthodox_Tewahedo_Church "Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church"), [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran"), [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist"), [Eastern Catholic](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic "Eastern Catholic"), [Schwarzenau (German Baptist) Brethren](/wiki/Schwarzenau_Brethren "Schwarzenau Brethren"),[[54]](#cite_note-55) [Mennonite](/wiki/Mennonite "Mennonite"), [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") and [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") traditions.[[55]](#cite_note-UMC_Maundy_Thursday-56)[[56]](#cite_note-Stamm2015-57)[[57]](#cite_note-Elwell2001-58)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Chancel_of_Houston_Memorial_UMC_on_Good_Friday.jpg/250px-Chancel_of_Houston_Memorial_UMC_on_Good_Friday.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chancel_of_Houston_Memorial_UMC_on_Good_Friday.jpg)

[Altar and crucifix veiled](/wiki/Stripping_of_the_Altar "Stripping of the Altar") in a Methodist church on Maundy Thursday in preparation for [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday")

With Maundy Thursday commemorating the [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper"), Christian denominations who observe this day universally celebrate the [sacrament](/wiki/Sacrament "Sacrament") of [Holy Communion](/wiki/Holy_Communion "Holy Communion"), which they teach was instituted by Jesus on this night.[[57]](#cite_note-Elwell2001-58) In the Catholic Church, the Lutheran Church and in certain Anglican congregations, the [Mass of the Lord's Supper](/wiki/Mass_of_the_Lord%27s_Supper "Mass of the Lord's Supper") begins as usual, but the [Gloria](/wiki/Gloria_in_Excelsis_Deo "Gloria in Excelsis Deo") is accompanied by the ringing of [church bells](/wiki/Church_bell "Church bell"), which are then silent until the [Easter Vigil](/wiki/Easter_Vigil "Easter Vigil").[[58]](#cite_note-CC-Maundy_Thursday-59)[[59]](#cite_note-Gramenz2021-60) After the homily the washing of feet may be performed. The [Blessed Sacrament](/wiki/Blessed_Sacrament "Blessed Sacrament") remains exposed, at least in the Catholic Mass, until the service concludes with a [procession](/wiki/Procession "Procession") taking it to the [place of reposition](/wiki/Altar_of_Repose "Altar of Repose"). The altar is later stripped bare, as are all other altars in the church except the Altar of Repose. In pre-1970 editions, the [Roman Missal](/wiki/Roman_Missal "Roman Missal") of the Catholic Church envisages this being done ceremonially, to the accompaniment of [Psalms 21–22](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Psalms#21:1 "s:Bible (King James)/Psalms"),[[60]](#cite_note-61)[[61]](#cite_note-62) a practice which continues in the Lutheran churches and Anglican churches of Anglo-Catholic [churchmanship](/wiki/Churchmanship "Churchmanship").[[62]](#cite_note-LL2013-63) In other [Christian denominations](/wiki/Christian_denominations "Christian denominations"), such as the Methodist Churches, the stripping of the altar and other items on the [chancel](/wiki/Chancel "Chancel") also occurs, as a preparation for the somber [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday") service.[[63]](#cite_note-LBW-Maundy_Thursday-64) The stripping of the altar represents "the abandonment of Jesus by his disciples and the stripping of Jesus by the soldiers before his crucifixion."[[62]](#cite_note-LL2013-63)

#### Chrism Mass

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maundy_Thursday&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Chrism Mass")]

Main article: [Chrism Mass](/wiki/Chrism_Mass "Chrism Mass")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/P_Villanueva_Messe_im_Lateran.jpg/250px-P_Villanueva_Messe_im_Lateran.jpg)](/wiki/File:P_Villanueva_Messe_im_Lateran.jpg)

Chrism Mass in the [Lateran Basilica](/wiki/Lateran_Basilica "Lateran Basilica")

The Chrism Mass is a religious service held in Roman Catholicism, Lutheranism and Anglicanism.[[64]](#cite_note-ELCA2013-65)[[65]](#cite_note-Tennessee-66)

Maundy Thursday is notable for being the day on which the Chrism Mass is celebrated in each [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese"). Usually held in the diocesan [cathedral](/wiki/Cathedral "Cathedral"), it is generally held on the morning of Maundy Thursday, but may in some dioceses take place on another day during Holy Week.[[66]](#cite_note-67) The Mass is a celebration of the institution of the priesthood.[[64]](#cite_note-ELCA2013-65)[[67]](#cite_note-68)

During the Mass, those present are called to renew their baptismal promises. Priests/ministers and deacons also reaffirm their ministry by renewing the promises made at their ordination.[[68]](#cite_note-Jalbert-69)[[69]](#cite_note-70) The Mass takes its name from the blessing of the [holy oils](/wiki/Chrism "Chrism") used in the sacraments throughout the year, which are then given to priests to take back to their parishes.[[70]](#cite_note-71)

The service is a 1967 restoration of the rite recorded in the early 200s by the historian [Hippolytus](/wiki/Hippolytus_of_Rome "Hippolytus of Rome") who writes of a ceremony taking place during the Easter Vigil at which two holy oils were blessed and one was consecrated. In the decree renewing this rite [Pope Paul VI](/wiki/Pope_Paul_VI "Pope Paul VI") said, "The Chrism Mass is one of the principal expressions of the fullness of the bishop's priesthood and signifies the closeness of the priests with him."[[71]](#cite_note-Baltimore-72)

The Holy Oils are:

* [Chrism](/wiki/Chrism "Chrism") – used in the sacraments of [Baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism"), [Confirmation](/wiki/Confirmation_in_the_Catholic_Church "Confirmation in the Catholic Church") and [Holy Orders](/wiki/Holy_orders_in_the_Catholic_Church "Holy orders in the Catholic Church"), as well as for the consecration of altars and the dedication of churches.[[72]](#cite_note-73)
* the [oil of catechumens](/wiki/Oil_of_catechumens "Oil of catechumens") – also used in the sacrament of Baptism, and
* the oil of the sick – used in the rite of the [Anointing of the Sick](/wiki/Anointing_of_the_Sick "Anointing of the Sick")

The oil of the catechumens and chrism are used on the upcoming [Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday") at the [Easter Vigil](/wiki/Easter_Vigil "Easter Vigil"), for the [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism") and [confirmation](/wiki/Confirmation "Confirmation") of those entering the church. While the Oil of the Catechumens and the Oil of the Sick, are simply "blessed," the Sacred Chrism is "consecrated,". Holy chrism is a mixture of olive oil and balsam, an aromatic resin. Balsam is poured into the oil, which gives it a sweet smell intended to remind those who encounter it of the "odor of sanctity" to which those who are marked with it, are called to strive.[[68]](#cite_note-Jalbert-69)

With respect to Anglicanism, the 1979 *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")* (p. 307) calls for chrism to be consecrated by the bishop. In many dioceses, the consecration of chrism by the bishop may be done at a service of reaffirmation of ordination vows during Holy Week. During the Chrism Eucharist, the Bishop will bless the oils used throughout the next year for baptisms and healing. In addition, the Bishop and clergy in attendance will reaffirm their Ordination Vows.[[65]](#cite_note-Tennessee-66)

#### Plenary indulgence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maundy_Thursday&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Plenary indulgence")]

In the Roman Catholic Church, plenary [indulgence](/wiki/Indulgence "Indulgence") is obtained in the following cases:[[73]](#cite_note-74)

1. If during the solemn reservation of the Blessed Sacrament (typically on a side altar), which follows the Mass of the Lord's Supper, it is recited or sung the Eucharistic hymn *[Tantum ergo](/wiki/Tantum_ergo "Tantum ergo")*.
2. If it is adored the solemnly reserved Blessed Sacrament for a half hour.

### Eastern Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maundy_Thursday&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Eastern Christianity")]

#### Eastern Orthodoxy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maundy_Thursday&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Eastern Orthodoxy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Omovenie_nog.jpg/250px-Omovenie_nog.jpg)](/wiki/File:Omovenie_nog.jpg)

Orthodox [icon](/wiki/Icon "Icon") of Christ washing the feet of the Apostles (16th century, [Pskov](/wiki/Pskov "Pskov") school of [iconography](/wiki/Iconography "Iconography"))

In the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), the [liturgical colours](/wiki/Liturgical_colour "Liturgical colour") are brighter, white being common. On this day alone during Holy Week, the fast is relaxed to permit consumption of wine and oil.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The primary service of this day is [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers") combined with the [Liturgy of St. Basil the Great](/wiki/Liturgy_of_St._Basil "Liturgy of St. Basil") at which is read a Composite Gospel, primarily taken from [Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew"), but with episodes inserted from [John](/wiki/Gospel_of_John "Gospel of John") ([the Washing of the Feet](/wiki/Maundy_(foot_washing) "Maundy (foot washing)")) and [Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke") ([Jesus sweating blood](/wiki/Hematidrosis#Instances "Hematidrosis")), and many of the normal hymns of the Divine Liturgy are substituted with the following [troparion](/wiki/Troparion "Troparion"):

> Of Thy Mystical Supper, O Son of God, accept me today as a communicant; for I will not speak of Thy [Mystery](/wiki/Sacred_Mysteries "Sacred Mysteries") to Thine enemies, neither will I give Thee a kiss like [Judas](/wiki/Judas "Judas"). But like the [Thief](/wiki/The_Good_Thief_(Christianity) "The Good Thief (Christianity)") will I confess Thee: Remember me, O Lord, in Thy [Kingdom](/wiki/Paradise "Paradise").[[74]](#cite_note-75)

When necessary to replenish the [sacrament for communing the sick at a time not following a divine liturgy](/wiki/Church_tabernacle#Reserved_sacrament "Church tabernacle"), an additional [Lamb (Host)](/wiki/Lamb_(Liturgy) "Lamb (Liturgy)") is consecrated on this day, [intincted](/wiki/Intinction#Eastern_Christianity "Intinction"), covered, and left to dry until Holy Saturday when it is divided, completely dried with a candle flame, and the pieces placed in the [artophorion](/wiki/Church_tabernacle#Communion_for_the_sick_–_Catholic_Rite "Church tabernacle").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In [cathedrals](/wiki/Cathedral "Cathedral") and [monasteries](/wiki/Monasteries "Monasteries") the ceremony of the [Washing of Feet](/wiki/Washing_of_Feet "Washing of Feet") is normally performed.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] When there is need to [consecrate](/wiki/Consecrate "Consecrate") more [chrism](/wiki/Chrism "Chrism"), that is performed by patriarchs and other heads of the various [autocephalous](/wiki/Autocephalous "Autocephalous") churches.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Lent_calendar.svg/250px-Lent_calendar.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Lent_calendar.svg)

Maunday Thursday and other named days and day ranges around Lent and Easter in Western Christianity, with the fasting days of Lent numbered

In the evening, after the Liturgy, all of the [hangings](/wiki/Antependia "Antependia") and [vestments](/wiki/Vestments "Vestments") are changed to black or some other dark colour, to signify the beginning of the Passion. Anticipating the Matins of Friday morning, the Holy Passion service of the reading of the **Twelve Gospels** is conducted. In these readings Christ's last instructions to his disciples are presented, as well as the prophecy of the drama of the Cross, Christ's prayer, and his new commandment. The twelve readings are:

* [John 18:1–29](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/John#18:1 "s:Bible (King James)/John")

Beginning on Holy and Great Thursday, the [memorial service for the dead](/wiki/Memorial_service_(Orthodox)#Lity "Memorial service (Orthodox)") is forbidden until after [Thomas Sunday](/wiki/Thomas_Sunday "Thomas Sunday").

##### Local customs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maundy_Thursday&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Local customs")]

* In Greek practice, the [Mystery](/wiki/Sacrament "Sacrament") of [Unction](/wiki/Unction "Unction") is performed on [Great Wednesday](/wiki/Holy_Wednesday "Holy Wednesday") as preparation for the reception of Holy Communion on Great Thursday and Pascha, a custom that originated when Greece was under [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") control and parish priests, being uneducated, were not permitted to hear confession, so this sacrament, by which sins are believed to be forgiven, came to be performed.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* In Greek tradition, a procession is made during the service of the Twelve Passion Gospels. It takes place after the reading of the fifth gospel during the singing of "Today He Who Hung". During this procession, a large cross with the body of Christ is carried throughout the church while lights are extinguished, bells are slowly tolled, and the faithful prostrate themselves. The cross, with Christ's body hung upon it, is placed in front of the Royal Doors. The icon of Christ on the cross (sometimes with nails affixing it) is struck upon the hands and feet with a stone multiple times, and is then stood up in front of the church, where it is censed.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* In some Slavic traditions, a lesser procession is made after the Twelve Passion Gospels immediately prior to the dismissal with an icon of Christ's crucifixion which is placed on the central icon stand, where it is censed by the clergy, and then venerated.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Oriental Orthodoxy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maundy_Thursday&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Oriental Orthodoxy")]

[Oriental Orthodox Churches](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches") celebrate Covenant Thursday during Holy Week (based on their local calendars). They offer praises and vespers every day of week, and on Thursday and Saturday they remain in church praising God and reading about the First Liturgy and Judas' Betrayal. On Saturday they sing praises for the Entrance of the Messiah in Hades freeing all of the faithful ones including Moses and Abraham.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Customs and names around the world

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maundy_Thursday&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Customs and names around the world")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Bishop_Sebouh_-_Washing_of_Feet.jpg/250px-Bishop_Sebouh_-_Washing_of_Feet.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bishop_Sebouh_-_Washing_of_Feet.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/BlessingChrism.jpg/250px-BlessingChrism.jpg)](/wiki/File:BlessingChrism.jpg)

Blessing Chrism

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Pesaha_at_Payyappilly%27s.jpg/250px-Pesaha_at_Payyappilly%27s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pesaha_at_Payyappilly%27s.jpg)

Pesaha appam (unleavened bread) and Pesaha milk made during Maundy Thursday by [Saint Thomas Christians](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christians "Saint Thomas Christians") of [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala"), [India](/wiki/India "India")

* If statues and crucifixes have been covered during [Passiontide](/wiki/Passiontide "Passiontide") (the last 2 weeks of Lent, at least in the 1962 Catholic missal), the crucifix covers are allowed to be white instead of purple for Holy Thursday.
* The popular [German](/wiki/German_(language) "German (language)") name *Gründonnerstag* means either "mourning Thursday" or "green Thursday".[[75]](#cite_note-76)
* In the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic") and [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia") the day is called *Zelený čtvrtek* or *Zelený štvrtok* respectively, again meaning "Green Thursday", because the typical meals of this day were made of fresh, green vegetables etc. From that day there is no usage of the [church bells](/wiki/Church_bell "Church bell") until [Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday"), here called "White Saturday", because "they have flown to [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome")" (a euphemism); in some regions they are replaced by groups of children walking round their village (or around the church) and making noise with wooden rattles. This is to announce to the people approaching beginning of the liturgy and to call the people to church.
* The tradition of silent bells also occurs in [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg"): the bells fall silent until Easter, because "they have flown to Rome for Confession", so children take to the streets, calling people to church with melancholy wooden rattling.[[76]](#cite_note-77)
* In the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") and [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), the day is called *Witte Donderdag* (White Thursday) referring to the [liturgical colour](/wiki/Liturgical_colour "Liturgical colour") of the day.
* In [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta"), Holy Thursday is known as *Ħamis ix-Xirka* (Communion Thursday) and the tradition of [visiting seven churches](/wiki/Seven_Churches_Visitation "Seven Churches Visitation") (see below) is called *is-seba' visti* or is-Sepulkri.
* [Eastern Slavic cultures](/wiki/Eastern_Slavic_peoples "Eastern Slavic peoples") traditionally carried out a range of cleansing ceremonies on Maundy Thursday (sometimes known in Russian as "Clean Thursday" ([Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"): Чистый четверг)).[[77]](#cite_note-78)
* In [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"), Maundy Thursday is *Dydd Iau Cablyd*.
* In [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") Maundy Thursday (skärtorsdagen) is connected to old folklore as the day of the witches. Young children often dress up as witches and knock on doors getting coins or candy for Easter eggs.
* In [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria") Maundy Thursday is called *Veliki Chetvurtuk* (Great Thursday), and is traditionally the day when people color their Easter eggs and perform other household chores geared toward preparing for *Razpeti Petuk* (Crucifixion Friday), *Velika Subota* (Great Saturday) and *Velikden* (Easter Day).
* In [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") the day is called as *[Pesaha](/wiki/Pesaha "Pesaha")* (പെസഹ), a [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam_language "Malayalam language") word derived from the Aramaic or Syriac word "*Peshai*" meaning [Passover](/wiki/Passover "Passover") . Maundy Thursday and following Good Friday are observed as statewide public holiday declared by the [Government of Kerala](/wiki/Government_of_Kerala "Government of Kerala"), given the high number of [Syrian Christians](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christians "Saint Thomas Christians") in the state.[[78]](#cite_note-newindianexpress-79) The tradition of consuming *[Pesaha appam](/wiki/Pesaha_appam "Pesaha appam")* or *Indariyappam* is customary after special longer [Holy Qurbana](/wiki/Holy_Qurbana "Holy Qurbana"), which are conducted on the or at midnight till morning in churches of [Saint Thomas Christians](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christians "Saint Thomas Christians"). On the evening before Good Friday the Pesaha bread is made at home. It is made with unleavened flour and they use a sweet drink call *Pesaha Paal* made up of coconut milk and jaggery along with this bread. On the Pesaha night the bread is steamed in a new vessel, immediately after rice flour is mixed with water and they pierce it many times with handle of the spoon to let out the steam so that the bread will not rise. This bread is cut by the head of the family and shared among the family members after prayers. In some families, *Pesaha Paal* a creamy dip made up of jaggery and coconut milk is used along with the Pesaha bread. If the family is in mourning following a death, Pesaha bread is not made at their home, but some of the Saint Thomas Christian neighbours share their bread with them.[[79]](#cite_note-80) The Saint Thomas Christians diaspora also celebrate this day by having Holy Communion services in the parishes according to their respective liturgies. The tradition of washing feet by priests is practiced in every parish commemorating [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") washing the feet of his disciples symbolizing humility.
* In the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), the day is officially known as *Huwebes Santo* (phonetic transliteration of Jueves Santo in Spanish, Holy Thursday) or "Maundy Thursday". Most businesses are closed during the [Easter Triduum](/wiki/Easter_Triduum "Easter Triduum"), with shopping malls opening on Black Saturday. Terrestrial television and radio stations either go completely off-air during the Triduum or operate on shorter hours with special programming; cable channels usually retain their normal programming. Newspapers do not publish on Good Friday and Black Saturday.

### Public holiday

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maundy_Thursday&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Public holiday")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/ChristWashingFeet.JPG/250px-ChristWashingFeet.JPG)](/wiki/File:ChristWashingFeet.JPG)

*Christus*, by the Lutheran [Lucas Cranach the Elder](/wiki/Lucas_Cranach_the_Elder "Lucas Cranach the Elder"). This woodcut of [John 13:14–17](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=John%2013:14–17&version=nrsv) is from *Passionary of the Christ and Antichrist*.

Maundy Thursday is a public holiday in most countries that were part of the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") ([Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"),[[80]](#cite_note-81) [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia"), [Costa Rica](/wiki/Costa_Rica "Costa Rica"), [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador"), [Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemala "Guatemala"), [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras"), [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), [Nicaragua](/wiki/Nicaragua "Nicaragua"), [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay "Paraguay"), [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru"), the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"),[[81]](#cite_note-82) [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay") and [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela")), countries that were part of the [Danish colonial empire](/wiki/Danish_colonial_empire "Danish colonial empire") ([Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"),[[82]](#cite_note-83) [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland"), [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") and [United States Virgin Islands](/wiki/United_States_Virgin_Islands "United States Virgin Islands")), and in the [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") State of [India](/wiki/India "India").
Certain German states declare a public holiday for public sector employees. In the UK, civil servants were traditionally granted a half-day holiday (known as "privilege leave") on this date, but that was abolished, by David Cameron, after 2012.

### Seven Churches Visitation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maundy_Thursday&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Seven Churches Visitation")]

Main article: [Seven Churches Visitation](/wiki/Seven_Churches_Visitation "Seven Churches Visitation")

The tradition of visiting seven churches on Holy Thursday is an ancient practice, probably originating in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome").[[83]](#cite_note-Hill-84) and occurs among the faithful in countries around the world.

In [India](/wiki/India "India"), the custom is to visit fourteen churches, one per [Station of the Cross](/wiki/Station_of_the_Cross "Station of the Cross"). Traditionally, this is performed on Maundy Thursday evening but is more often done on the morning of Good Friday or on any day of Lent. Usually, whole families would participate, customarily [fasting](/wiki/Fasting_and_abstinence_in_the_Roman_Catholic_Church "Fasting and abstinence in the Roman Catholic Church") for the duration of the rite. It is also undertaken by parish devotional groups.

In the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") the tradition is called *[Visita Iglesia](/wiki/Visita_Iglesia "Visita Iglesia")* ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language_in_the_Philippines "Spanish language in the Philippines"), "church visit") – people visit churches to pray, usually reciting the [Stations of the Cross](/wiki/Stations_of_the_Cross "Stations of the Cross"). It is a chiefly urban custom, as churches are located closer to each other in [cities](/wiki/Cities_of_the_Philippines "Cities of the Philippines"), and supposedly because it originates in visiting the seven churches of [Intramuros](/wiki/Intramuros "Intramuros") that stood until the 1945 [Bombing of Manila](/wiki/Bombing_of_Manila "Bombing of Manila").[[84]](#cite_note-85)
The original purpose of the ritual was to venerate the [Blessed Sacrament](/wiki/Blessed_Sacrament "Blessed Sacrament") in the [Altar of Repose](/wiki/Altar_of_Repose "Altar of Repose") on Maundy Thursday night, but since no prayers were prescribed (apart from those for the [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope")), the Stations of the Cross were recited.

In [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), the visiting of churches occurs shortly after the evening Mass of the Lord's Supper. Prayers at each church consist of seven repetitions of the [Lord's Prayer](/wiki/Lord%27s_Prayer "Lord's Prayer"), [Ave Maria](/wiki/Ave_Maria "Ave Maria"), and the *[Gloria Patri](/wiki/Gloria_Patri "Gloria Patri")*. Due to the new trend of late Mass times (sometimes 7 or 8 pm) to allow for more churchgoers, eight churches are the maximum number visited (even in the city area, where these are closer to each other than in outer residential areas) before these close at midnight. A festive atmosphere exists, with the sale of drinks, [hot cross buns](/wiki/Hot_cross_bun "Hot cross bun") and other local snacks like the traditional *kueh ko chee*. Observant Catholics have a 'Last Supper' meal in anticipation of the next day's fast.
